column1:
Why should a venous insufficiency exam be performed with the patient in the standing position?

A: To evaluate the veins with the added effect of hydrostatic pressure
B: To ensure the valve leaflets are closed during the exam
C: To increase the effects of the Valsalva maneuver
D: To engorge the veins to make them easier to identify with ultrasound
column2:
Answer: A. To evaluate the veins with the added effect of hydrostatic pressure

Feedback:
A venous insufficiency exam should be performed with the patient in the standing position to evaluate the veins with the added effect of hydrostatic pressure. This will allow reflux to more easily be identified if it is present.

column1:
In most patients the right common carotid artery:

A: bifurcates at the level if the clavicle
B: bifurcates at a lower level in the neck than the left CCA
C: is nearly twice the diameter of the left CCA
D: originates directly from the aortic arch
column2:
Answer: B. bifurcates at a lower level in the neck than the left CCA

Feedback:
Right CCA originates from the innominate artery. Left CCA originates from the aortic arch (second branch from arch). In most patients the right CCA bifurcates at a lower level than the left CCA.

column1:
If a patient has a history of 80% stenosis of the left internal carotid artery identified on an MRA exam. Which of the following Doppler velocities from the ICA will correlate well with the predicted level of stenosis?

A: PSV 200cm/s, EDV 100cm/s
B: PSV 300cm/s, EDV 150cm/s
C: PSV 200cm/s, EDV 80cm/s
D: PSV 125cm/s, EDV 40cm/s
column2:
Answer: B. PSV 300cm/s, EDV 150cm/s

Feedback:
Strandness Criteria: 80-99% stenosis = PSV >125cm/s, EDV >140cm/s SRU Criteria >70% stenosis = PSV >230cm/s, EDV >100cm/s ICAVL Criteria >70% stenosis = PSV >230cm/s, EDV >100cm/s

column1:
A patient's carotid exam demonstrates 50-69% stenosis. What will be the recommended treatment for this patient?

A: 6-month follow up exam
B: angiography
C: endarterectomy
D: stent
column2:
Answer: A. 6-month follow up exam

Feedback:
If the exam indicates a 50-69% stenosis, a 6-month follow up exam will be needed. When the stenosis reaches 70% or greater, treatment options will be considered.

column1:
Which of the following will be administered to a patient with extensive DVT to cause lysis of the clot?

A: Heparin or Coumadin
B: Coumadin
C: Heparin
D: Streptokinase
column2:
Answer: D. Streptokinase

Feedback:
Heparin, Coumadin and Warfarin are medications that reduce the viscosity of blood to prevent propagation of the clot, but they have no clot lysing properties. Streptokinase and Urokinase are used to dissolve the thrombus in cases with significant thrombosis of the venous system.

column1:
If the systemic blood pressure rises but flow remains constant, what happens to the resistance in the vascular beds?

A: increases
B: unable to determine without knowing the flow velocity
C: no change
D: decreases
column2:
Answer: A. increases

Feedback:
If Flow Volume (Q) remains the same, if pressure (P) changes, resistance (R) must also change to keep the same flow volume. Resistance and pressure are directly related. If pressure goes up, resistance must go up to maintain a consistent amount of flow volume. Simplified Mathematic Example: Flow Volume (Q) 6 = (P) 12 / (R) 2 Pressure goes up to 24, Resistance must increase to 4 to calculate the same flow volume of 6. (24 / 4 = 6)

column1:
Which of the following is a common occurrence after an endarterectomy?

A: Intracranial hemorrhage
B: Intimal dissection
C: Neointimal hyperplasia
D: Congestive heart failure
column2:
Answer: C. Neointimal hyperplasia

Feedback:
Neointimal hyperplasia refers to the overgrowth of normal tissue in response to the endarterectomy. This is a common cause of re-stenosis of a vessel that previously underwent endarterectomy.

column1:
Which of the following is a treatment for a pseudoaneurysm?

A: Thrombin injection
B: Coumadin
C: Compression stockings
D: IV Heparin
column2:
Answer: A. Thrombin injection

Feedback:
A Thrombin injection is used to treat pseudoaneurysms with large stalks. Ultrasound guidance is used to make the injection that promotes thrombosis of the stalk and body of the pseudoaneurysm. Coumadin is a blood thinner that would decrease the ability of clot formation in the pseudoaneurysm. IV Heparin is used to treat DVT.

column1:
Where is the most common location of stenosis in a hemodialysis graft?

A: Inflow artery
B: mid graft, especially if it is a looped graft
C: Arterial anastomosis and proximal graft
D: Venous anastomosis and outflow vein
column2:
Answer: D. Venous anastomosis and outflow vein

Feedback:
The venous outflow is the most common location of stenosis in an AVG for dialysis. Because the vein wall is thinner and receives pulsatile arterial flow, the wall is easily damaged. This can lead to repetitive healing and scar tissue formation that causes blockage at the anastomosis.

column1:
Which of the following correctly describes thoracic outlet syndrome?

A: PPG or PVR tracings can be used to evaluate a patient with suspected TOS
B: Patients commonly experiencing dull, aching pain and paresthesia in the affected knee and ankle
C: The Homan sign indicates a positive test result for TOS.
D: The Tinel test is used during assessment for TOS.
column2:
Answer: A. PPG or PVR tracings can be used to evaluate a patient with suspected TOS

Feedback:
Patients with TOS commonly experience dull, aching pain and paresthesia in the affected shoulder/arm. PPG or PVR tracings can be used to evaluate a patient with suspected TOS using specific maneuvers during testing. The Homan sign refers to pain in the calf with quick dorsiflexion of the foot, seen with DVT. The Tinel sign indicates a damaged nerve at the wrist and is associated with hypothenar hammer syndrome.

column1:
According to the findings on this arteriogram, which vessel will demonstrate absent or abnormal flow pattern on a carotid Duplex exam?

A: R and L vertebral arteries and R Subclavian
B: R Subclavian and R CCA
C: LCCA, L Vertebral, L Subclavian
D: R Vertebral and R Subclavian
column2:
Answer: C. LCCA, L Vertebral, L Subclavian

Feedback:
There is a significant stenosis of the left CCA and an occlusion of the proximal left subclavian. The left CCA will demonstrate post-stenotic turbulence and elevated velocity in the proximal segment. There is no flow demonstrated in the left subclavian or vertebral artery. NO steal is present or these vessels would be identified on the image. **Image Credit Bronchiectasis and hoarseness of voice in takayasu arteritis: a rare presentation. Nd Perera G, C Jayasinghe A, D Dias L, Kulatunga A - BMC Res Notes (2012)

column1:
The dorsalis pedis artery is a branch of:

A: the peroneal artery
B: the posterior tibial artery
C: the anterior tibial artery
D: the gastrocnemius artery
column2:
Answer: C. the anterior tibial artery

Feedback:
The DPA is a distal branch of the ATA.

column1:
Which artery supplies the brainstem with arterial blood?

A: basilar artery
B: anterior cerebral artery
C: posterior cerebral artery
D: posterior communicating artery
column2:
Answer: A. basilar artery

Feedback:
Branches from the vertebral and basilar arteries feed the cerebellum and portions of the brain stem.

column1:
Broca aphasia can be defined as:

A: Inability to understand speech, speak, or follow directions
B: Inability to swallow solid food
C: Inability to speak but can understand and follow directions
D: Inability to swallow liquids
column2:
Answer: C. Inability to speak but can understand and follow directions

Feedback:
Broca aphasia (AKA expressive aphasia) can be defined as the inability to speak but can understand and follow directions.

column1:
Exercise causes ________________ which leads to _________________ in the lower extremities.

A: vasoconstriction, increased resistance
B: vasodilation, decreased resistance
C: vasoconstriction, decreased resistance
D: vasodilation, increased resistance
column2:
Answer: B. vasodilation, decreased resistance

Feedback:
Vasodilation occurs and resistance to flow drops to encourage more flow volume distally, as the muscles require more oxygen with exercise. The resistance to flow decreases and pressure increases with exercise to increase the flow volume to the functioning muscles. If enough blood does not reach the muscles, symptoms like claudication can occur.

column1:
The image is a transverse view of right shoulder. What vessel is indicated by letter A?

A: superior vena cava
B: subclavian vein
C: axillary vein
D: innominate vein
column2:
Answer: C. axillary vein

Feedback:

column1:
Vasodilation:

A: can cause a monophasic waveform to become triphasic
B: usually occurs in response to cold stimuli
C: usually occurs with stress or anxiety
D: can cause a triphasic waveform to become biphasic
column2:
Answer: D. can cause a triphasic waveform to become biphasic

Feedback:
Vasodilation causes reduced resistance to flow to meet the tissue demands. Exercise will cause vasodilation and a triphasic waveform can become biphasic with the reduced resistance to flow in the lower extremities. The resistance to flow decreases and pressure increases with exercise to increase the flow volume to the functioning muscles. If enough blood does not reach the muscles, symptoms like claudication can occur.

column1:
Which of the following veins has a direct connection to both superficial and deep veins?

A: popliteal vein and inferior vena cava
B: brachial vein and small saphenous vein
C: femoral vein and axillary vein
D: subclavian and popliteal vein
column2:
Answer: D. subclavian and popliteal vein

Feedback:
The subclavian vein receives blood from the cephalic vein and the axillary vein. The popliteal vein receives blood from the small saphenous and deep calf veins.

column1:
Which of the following is performed with the patient sitting upright?

A: PVR evaluation of the toes
B: pressure evaluation of the fingers
C: pressure evaluation of the toes
D: penile Doppler exam
column2:
Answer: B. pressure evaluation of the fingers

Feedback:
Finger pressures should be obtained with the patient sitting upright, hand placed on a positioning wedge or pillow.

column1:
The blue arrow indicates which of the following structures?

A: Ophthalmic
B: Superior thyroidal artery
C: Internal thoracic artery
D: Thyrocervical trunk
column2:
Answer: B. Superior thyroidal artery

Feedback:
The superior thyroidal artery is the first branch of the ECA. Compare the size of the two vessels imaged and their anatomic location. The smaller, more medial vessel demonstrates the branch coursing inferiorly into the neck to the thyroid.

column1:
What effect does a warm exam room have on a vein mapping procedure performed for coronary bypass surgery?

A: contracted veins with reduced diameters
B: normal venous diameters will be documented
C: reduced compressibility of the superficial veins
D: increase in tissue edema and reduced image resolution
column2:
Answer: B. normal venous diameters will be documented

Feedback:
When performing a vein mapping, the exam room should be kept warm in order to limit vasoconstriction. During the exam, cover the rest of the patient and the foot/hand to keep warm. If the veins demonstrate thickened walls and reduced diameter, warm the leg or evaluate a different vein.

column1:
When a water hammer pulse is identified in the carotid arteries, what cardiac complication should be suspected?

A: severe tricuspid regurgitation
B: severe aortic regurgitation
C: severe mitral stenosis
D: severe mitral regurgitation
column2:
Answer: B. severe aortic regurgitation

Feedback:
Blood moves back through the aortic valve into the left ventricle during diastole. It is usually caused by a diseased valve, congenital defects and chamber or aortic dilatation. Significant regurgitation results in volume overload in the chamber receiving the retrograde blood flow. With aortic regurgitation, volume overload occurs in the left ventricle. Pulsus bisferiens can occur in the carotid arteries. The waveforms will demonstrate double systolic peaks. Severe regurgitation is associated with diastolic flow reversal in the carotid arteries (Water Hammer Pulse).

column1:
The transducer is placed in the longitudinal position slightly anterior to midline of the medial upper left calf. The beam is angled posterior until the image displayed appears. What vessel is indicated by letter B?

A: peroneal vein
B: anterior tibial vein
C: posterior tibial vein
D: gastrocnemius vein
column2:
Answer: A. peroneal vein

Feedback:
The image displays the confluence of the posterior tibial and peroneal veins. The peroneal veins are deeper on the image.

column1:
The patient being evaluated has a known cardiac arrhythmia. How would you adjust the Doppler controls to best display the arrhythmia?

A: increase the sample size
B: reduce the wall filter setting
C: increase the PW Doppler scale
D: reduce the sweep speed
column2:
Answer: D. reduce the sweep speed

Feedback:
When a cardiac arrhythmia is present, the sweep speed should be reduced to demonstrate more cardiac cycles on the tracing. This will allow you to show a few normal cycles before and after the cycle affected by the arrhythmia.

column1:
What is the preferred patient position for performing an upper extremity arterial duplex exam?

A: arm abducted 90 degrees and externally rotated
B: arm raised above head with patient in supine position
C: arm abducted 45 degrees and externally rotated
D: arm adducted 45 degrees and internally rotated
column2:
Answer: C. arm abducted 45 degrees and externally rotated

Feedback:
The preferred patient position for performing an upper extremity arterial duplex exam is to abduct the arm 45 degrees with external rotation.

column1:
Which of the following correctly describes an aortic aneurysm?

A: Patients with a popliteal artery aneurysm rarely have an associated AAA
B: An AAA with a diameter of 4.5cm or greater will require surgical intervention
C: The most common complication of an AAA is thrombus formation in the IVC
D: The AP diameter is considered the most reliable dimension for diagnosis of an AAA
column2:
Answer: D. The AP diameter is considered the most reliable dimension for diagnosis of an AAA

Feedback:
The AP diameter is considered the most reliable dimension for diagnosis of an AAA. An AAA with a diameter of 5.5cm or greater will require surgical intervention. The most common complication of a AAA is rupture. >60% of patients that have a popliteal artery aneurysm also have an AAA.

column1:
Which of the following describes a difference between endovenous ablation of the great saphenous vein and of the small saphenous vein?

A: the catheter is inserted at the groin for the treatment of the GSV and at the dorsum of the foot for the treatment of the SSV
B: at the start of the procedure, the patient is placed in the supine position for the GSV treatment and in the prone position for the SSV treatment
C: The SSV cannot be treated by endovenous ablation techniques
D: the catheter is inserted at the groin for the treatment of the GSV and at the popliteal fossa for the treatment of the SSV
column2:
Answer: B. at the start of the procedure, the patient is placed in the supine position for the GSV treatment and in the prone position for the SSV treatment

Feedback:
The patient is placed in the supine position for the GSV treatment and in the prone position for the SSV treatment. The catheter is inserted in the distal GSV and advanced to a position 2cm from the SFJ. The catheter is inserted in the distal SSV and advanced to a position distal to the SPJ. The table position will be adjusted to Trendelenburg position after the catheter is inserted to drain excess blood from the veins before device activation. Once activated the catheter is pulled down the vein and out the puncture site.

column1:
Why should a segmental pressure exam be performed with the patient supine?

A: To eliminate the influence of hydrostatic pressure on the brachial pressures
B: To provide the most comfortable position for the patient during the lengthy exam
C: To increase the pressure in the venous system and reduce the pressures in the arterial system
D: To eliminate the influence of hydrostatic pressure on the ankle pressures
column2:
Answer: D. To eliminate the influence of hydrostatic pressure on the ankle pressures

Feedback:
Hydrostatic pressure can cause an overestimation of pressures in the ankles. Segmental pressure evaluations should be performed with the patient supine.

column1:
If flow in the left ophthalmic artery is reversed in a transorbital evaluation, what is the expected flow pattern in the right ophthalmic artery?

A: flow moving away from the transducer
B: increased pulsatility
C: reduced systolic and diastolic velocities
D: increased systolic and diastolic velocities
column2:
Answer: D. increased systolic and diastolic velocities

Feedback:
Normal flow in the ophthalmic artery should be moving toward the transducer in the transorbital window. If flow is reversed in the left ophthalmic artery, the right ophthalmic artery will demonstrate increased antegrade flow volume and velocities with decreased pulsatility.

column1:
You are about to perform a lower extremity arterial duplex on a patient that recently had an MRA run-off exam. According to the attached image, what vessel will demonstrate reduced or absent flow in the right leg?

A: anterior tibial artery
B: common femoral artery
C: posterior tibial artery
D: superficial femoral artery
column2:
Answer: D. superficial femoral artery

Feedback:
Note the limited display of contrast in the superficial femoral artery of the right leg and the anterior tibial artery of the left leg. You should expect that Doppler evaluation of these vessels will demonstrate little to no flow.

column1:
What venous valves are closed in the lower extremity during calf muscle relaxation?

A: Proximal thigh veins
B: Calf veins
C: All of the valves in the deep and superficial system
D: All of the valves in the deep system
column2:
Answer: A. Proximal thigh veins

Feedback:
During muscle contraction, the large blood volume residing in the deep calf veins and soleal sinuses is squeezed up and out of the calf. The proximal valves of the deep veins are forced open. The distal valves close to prevent caudal flow. The perforator valves close to prevent flow into the superficial venous system. During calf muscle relaxation, the proximal valves close due to the hydrostatic pressure forcing blood back down the veins to the level of the valve. During relaxation, the distal and perforator valves open, and blood flows into the calf deep veins.

column1:
Which of the following portions of the leg would be swollen with chronic insufficiency?

A: calf only
B: ankle and calf, but not the foot
C: foot and ankle, but not the calf
D: calf, foot, ankle
column2:
Answer: B. ankle and calf, but not the foot

Feedback:
Swelling of the feet normally does not occur with venous disease (systemic or cardiac disease, yes)

column1:
When scanning the internal carotid, which way would you angle your probe to locate the external carotid artery in most patients?

A: superior and/or lateral
B: 40 degrees medially
C: medial and/or anterior
D: lateral and/or anterior
column2:
Answer: C. medial and/or anterior

Feedback:
When scanning the internal carotid artery, you angle your probe medial and anterior to locate the external carotid artery in most patients

column1:
Which of the following is an expected change in flow in the brachial artery that occurs when the patient clenches their fist during the Doppler exam?

A: decreased resistive index
B: increased diastolic flow
C: triphasic to monophasic waveform change
D: increased pulsatility index
column2:
Answer: D. increased pulsatility index

Feedback:
Clenching the fist causes an increase in distal resistance. The brachial artery will show increased pulsatility and resistance with decreased diastolic flow. Isometric flexing of the foot/toes has the same effect on the Doppler evaluation of the lower extremity arteries. Note the increased resistance to flow with the loss of the antegrade flow in diastole. Higher resistance waveforms exhibit greater pulsatility index.

column1:
Localized redness and warmth of the distal calf is most commonly associated with:

A: Cellulitis
B: Acute DVT formation in the popliteal vein
C: Acute arterial occlusion of the popliteal artery
D: Anterior compartment syndrome
column2:
Answer: A. Cellulitis

Feedback:
Redness and warmth are most commonly seen with cellulitis but can be a symptom of acute DVT.

column1:
Which of the following describes the abnormal response to papaverine injection during a penile impotence exam?

A: increased reflux in the veins in the scrotal varicocele
B: the PSV and EDV of the arterial inflow will decrease
C: the PSV and EDV will increase post injection, but dorsal venous flow will remain unchanged
D: Decreased dorsal venous outflow
column2:
Answer: B. the PSV and EDV of the arterial inflow will decrease

Feedback:
Doppler evaluation includes assessment of the cavernous arteries with Doppler pre- and post- medication injection (papaverine most common); normally the PSV and EDV will increase post injection but dorsal venous flow will remain unchanged.

column1:
Air in a patch used during a carotid endarterectomy can degrade the evaluation of the treated area in the immediate post-op setting. What is the recommended approach for evaluating these patients?

A: use sterile gel and scan directly over the incision
B: use the most posterior approach possible
C: use the most anterior approach possible
D: patients with a recent endarterectomy should not be evaluated with ultrasound within the first 14 days after the procedure
column2:
Answer: B. use the most posterior approach possible

Feedback:
If ultrasound is performed soon after the procedure, artifacts produced by air in the patch or graft may inhibit the exam. To minimize artifact from air in the patch, use the most posterior approach possible to evaluate the bifurcation.

column1:
Increased peripheral resistance will lead to______________ amplitude in PVR tracings.

A: increased
B: decreased
C: little change in
D: the formation of a second dicrotic notch
column2:
Answer: A. increased

Feedback:
Increased peripheral resistance will lead to increased amplitude in PVR tracings. Proximal disease will lead to a reduced amplitude and loss of the dicrotic notch.

column1:
Letter I indicates which of the following vessels?

A: left hepatic vein
B: left portal vein
C: right portal vein
D: right hepatic vein
column2:
Answer: D. right hepatic vein

Feedback:
I. right hepatic vein J. middle hepatic vein K. left hepatic vein

column1:
The profunda artery demonstrates a 75% stenosis. Which of the following arteries will demonstrate monophasic flow due to the stenosis?

A: popliteal
B: posterior tibial
C: anterior tibial
D: none of the above
column2:
Answer: D. none of the above

Feedback:
The femoral artery supplies the popliteal and calf vessels with blood. A stenosis in the profunda artery will not cause decreased resistance in the vessels of the lower leg.

column1:
The following pressure readings from a segmental pressure evaluation were obtained in a patient suffering from low back and thigh pain. What is the ABI for each leg? Lt Ankle 136 Rt Ankle 128 Lt Arm 124 Rt Arm 120

A: Rt = 1.07, Lt = 1.10
B: Rt = 0.94, Lt = 0.91
C: Rt = 1.03, Lt = 1.13
D: Rt = 1.03, Lt = 1.10
column2:
Answer: D. Rt = 1.03, Lt = 1.10

Feedback:
Rt ABI = 128/124 = 1.03 Lt ABI = 136/124 = 1.10 You should not have complicated math problems on the registry exam. The most important part of any calculation is to know how to set up the calculation. The highest calf artery pressure is divided by the highest brachial pressure to calculate the ABI.

column1:
A fem - tib graft is used to treat:

A: unilateral iliac stenosis
B: bilateral popliteal stenosis
C: bilateral iliac stenosis
D: unilateral distal femoral stenosis
column2:
Answer: D. unilateral distal femoral stenosis

Feedback:
A fem - tib graft can be used to correct unilateral distal femoral or popliteal stenosis. The femoral artery is connected by a graft to the anterior tibial artery or tibioperoneal trunk distal to the obstruction.

column1:
Congestive heart failure causes increased pulsatility in the:

A: bilateral superficial femoral arteries
B: renal arteries and veins
C: bilateral carotid arteries
D: iliac veins and portal veins
column2:
Answer: D. iliac veins and portal veins

Feedback:
CHF causes reduced cardiac output and a backlog if flow in the venous system. The systemic and portal venous system can demonstrate increased pulsatility with CHF. The decreased cardiac output can reduce pulsatility in the distal arterial system.

column1:
Aspirin would be the preferred treatment for which of the following patients?

A: 60% internal carotid stenosis
B: presence of significant venous reflux
C: total occlusion of an internal carotid artery
D: compartment syndrome
column2:
Answer: A. 60% internal carotid stenosis

Feedback:
Aspirin prevents platelet aggregation in the arteries which reduces the ability of plaque to form. Patients with mild to moderate carotid stenosis can benefit from an aspirin each day.

column1:
The popliteal artery ___________ the popliteal vein.

A: courses anterior to
B: is more tortuous than
C: is larger than
D: courses posterior to
column2:
Answer: A. courses anterior to

Feedback:
The popliteal artery courses anterior to the popliteal vein. When answering questions regarding anatomic relationships and considering US images, always start by considering the transducer placement on the body. The popliteal vein is imaged by placing the transducer in the posterior popliteal fossa. The anterior aspect of this image is the posterior aspect of the patient.

column1:
A patient presents with HTN, DM and left lower extremity pain that occurs only as he walks. He usually begins to have pain after he walks to his mailbox with increasing pain as he returns to the house. The pain is relieved by resting his legs for a few minutes. What would the predicted ABI value be for the left leg?

A: 0.9
B: 0.6
C: 0.4
D: above 1.0; these symptoms are most likely unrelated to arterial disease
column2:
Answer: B. 0.6

Feedback:
The patient is experiencing claudication which is found in patients with 0.5 - 0.9 ABI. The short distance of walking to the mailbox before the pain begins indicates a more significant amount of disease than 0.9 ABI would indicate. An ABI of 0.6 is the best choice with the given symptoms.

column1:
An upper extremity segmental pressure exam produces the following pressures: Brachial RT 140mmHg AND LT 132mmHg Radial RT 144mmHg AND LT 136mmHg Ulnar RT 138mmHg AND LT 138mmHg 2nd Digit RT 130mmHg AND LT 104mmHg What should you do next for this patient?

A: end the exam and report right palmar arch disease
B: perform digital pressures on at least one more digit on the right hand
C: perform digital pressures on at least one more digit on the left hand
D: end the exam and report left palmar arch disease
column2:
Answer: C. perform digital pressures on at least one more digit on the left hand

Feedback:
When the arm pressures are normal, but the digital pressure is abnormally reduced, there could be disease in the palmar arch or the single digit. To provide a complete evaluation, at least one more digit on the left hand should be evaluated. If the additional digit pressure is abnormal, disease in the palmar arch is the most likely cause for the reduced digit pressures. If the additional digit pressure is normal, the remaining digits should be tested to confirm that the reduced pressure is limited to the single digit and disease of that digital artery should be suspected.

column1:
What critical finding is associated with calf paresthesia, localized muscle weakness, pain when stretching calf muscles, and drop foot?

A: peripheral arterial disease
B: anterior compartment syndrome
C: popliteal entrapment
D: post-phlebitic syndrome
column2:
Answer: B. anterior compartment syndrome

Feedback:
Acute compartment syndrome is a medical emergency. It is usually caused by a severe injury and without intervention, it can lead to permanent muscle damage. Symptoms of compartment syndrome include paresthesia, localized muscle weakness, pain when stretching calf muscles, drop foot.

column1:
A patient presents 3 days after left hip surgery for a DVT evaluation. There is severe edema from the hip to the ankle and the leg has a mild cyanotic appearance. You identify acute thrombus from the common femoral vein through the calf veins. The great saphenous vein is also occluded by thrombus. What is the diagnosis for this condition?

A: Budd Chiari syndrome
B: Phlegmasia Cerulea Dolens
C: Phlegmasia Alba Dolens
D: Acute arterial occlusion
column2:
Answer: B. Phlegmasia Cerulea Dolens

Feedback:
Phlegmasia Cerulea Dolens refers to extensive DVT occluding deep system and superficial system. Arterial inflow is significantly affected. Severe limb edema and mottled skin are noted with cyanosis due to high concentration of deoxygenated blood. Tip for remembering the difference between Cerulea Dolens and Alba Dolens, "C"erulea causes "C"yanosis of the leg, while Alba Dolens results in pallor.

column1:
A 65yr old female presents chronic leg pain and swelling, but no brown discoloration is present in the distal calf and ankle area. Which of the following patient history questions would provide an explanation of the symptoms?

A: Do you have pain in the legs at night when in bed?
B: Does your pain increase with quick dorsiflexion of the left foot?
C: Have you had coronary artery bypass surgery?
D: If you elevate the leg, does the pain increase?
column2:
Answer: C. Have you had coronary artery bypass surgery?

Feedback:
The great saphenous is removed for most CABG procedures. The removal of this vessel can lead to swelling in the leg with the reduced outflow. Brown discoloration is not a common sign of GSV removal because the reduced outflow is in the deep veins and not related to insufficiency. Pain with quick dorsiflexion of the foot is a sign of DVT called Homan's sign. Pain increase with leg elevation or at night would indicate arterial disease, which does not correlate with swelling.

column1:
A carotid Doppler exam demonstrates an occlusion of the left ICA, but the distal ICA demonstrates retrograde flow. The ECA demonstrates antegrade flow with increased diastolic flow. The ophthalmic artery on the left side demonstrates flow moving away from the transducer. Where is the collateral flow coming from?

A: cross over collateralization through the ACA
B: ECA to ICA collateralization
C: vertebrobasilar collateralization
D: ophthalmic to ophthalmic collateralization
column2:
Answer: B. ECA to ICA collateralization

Feedback:
Flow reversal in the ophthalmic artery is a key sign of ECA to ICA collateral flow. If the ICA is obstructed proximally, collateral flow can refill the ICA distally. Flow moves from the left ECA - left superficial temporal artery - left supraorbital artery(reversed) - left ophthalmic artery(reversed) - distal left ICA (reversed)

column1:
Which of the following patients has the lowest risk of a future stroke that affects the right side?

A: total occlusion of the left ICA
B: 60% left ICA stenosis caused by heterogeneous calcific plaque
C: 20% left ICA stenosis caused by smooth, soft plaque
D: no appreciable plaque identified in the left ICA
column2:
Answer: A. total occlusion of the left ICA

Feedback:
A stenosis on the left internal carotid artery will cause symptoms on the right side of the body. The risk of a future stroke on that side drops significantly when the internal carotid artery is totally occluded. If a stroke would occur, it would have happened at the time the occlusion occurred.

column1:
Which of the following describes a normal lower extremity response to the Valsalva maneuver?

A: the femoral vein will demonstrate cardiac pulsatility
B: flow in the femoral vein will stop at onset of the maneuver
C: flow in the subclavian vein will be augmented toward the heart with slight reversal upon release
D: flow in the iliac vein will reverse for at least 1 second before normalizing
column2:
Answer: B. flow in the femoral vein will stop at onset of the maneuver

Feedback:
The Valsalva maneuver will significantly decrease or stop flow toward the heart in the deep veins of the legs. Upon release of the maneuver, flow will be briefly augmented toward the heart and then normalize.

column1:
Which of the following is a sign of a functioning hemodialysis graft?

A: pain or pallor in the hand
B: palpable thrill over the graft
C: absence of a palpable thrill over the graft
D: presence of a water hammer pulse
column2:
Answer: B. palpable thrill over the graft

Feedback:
A normal AVG should demonstrate a palpable thrill as blood flow moves through it. Absence of the thrill or a water hammer pulse is indicative of an occluded graft. If the graft becomes stenosed or occluded, the distal forearm and hand could become ischemic.

column1:
An 80yr old female patient asks you to explain the purpose of a segmental pressure exam of the lower extremities. Which of the following is the best response?

A: Tell her you are going to squeeze her legs for a minute just relax.
B: Tell her that the pressure in your legs decreases with disease and the doctor wants to see how much her ankle pressures have dropped.
C: Tell her the exam uses blood pressure cuffs to take her blood pressure in her legs to evaluate the circulation to her feet.
D: Tell her the exam uses blood pressure cuffs to take her blood pressure in her legs to see if she needs surgery.
column2:
Answer: C. Tell her the exam uses blood pressure cuffs to take her blood pressure in her legs to evaluate the circulation to her feet.

Feedback:
Always tailor your explanation to your audience. Limit technical details that are not relevant for the patient. You should not suggest that surgery may be necessary or that her ankle pressures are decreased. As a Sonographer, you should always explain the exam and never be condescending or dismissive of a patient's request.

column1:
When removing a protective gown:

A: untie the strings, roll the gown upward from the bottom edge to turn it inside out, pull it from the shoulders and off the arms, remove your gloves, wash your hands
B: untie the strings, pull the gown forward from the shoulders using the neck strings to turn it inside out as it is removed, remove your gloves, wash your hands
C: remove your gloves, untie the strings, pull the gown forward from the center of the front of the gown to remove it with the exposed side out, roll it into a ball, wash your hands
D: remove your gloves, untie the strings, pull the gown forward from the shoulders using the neck strings, grab the inside shoulder seams to pull it off the arms and turn it inside out as it is removed, wash your hands
column2:
Answer: D. remove your gloves, untie the strings, pull the gown forward from the shoulders using the neck strings, grab the inside shoulder seams to pull it off the arms and turn it inside out as it is removed, wash your hands

Feedback:
When removing a protective gown, remove your gloves, untie the strings, pull the gown forward from the shoulders using the neck strings, grab the inside shoulder seams pull it off the arms and turn it inside out as it is removed, wash your hands.

column1:
When evaluating a focal stenosis of the femoral artery, where should you place the cursor to obtain the highest velocity related to the stenosis?

A: immediately proximal to the focal point of the stenosis
B: 1-2cm proximal to the focal point of the stenosis
C: at the narrowest point in the vessel or immediately distal to the focal point of the stenosis
D: 1-2cm distal to the focal point of the stenosis
column2:
Answer: C. at the narrowest point in the vessel or immediately distal to the focal point of the stenosis

Feedback:
When evaluating a focal stenosis of an artery, you should place the cursor at the narrowest point in the vessel or immediately distal to the focal point of the stenosis to obtain the highest velocity related to the stenosis. It is important to move the Doppler cursor through the stenotic area and out the other side to locate the peak velocity.

column1:
Which artery courses anteriorly along the calf and laterally through the interosseous membrane of the leg?

A: distal superficial femoral artery
B: posterior tibial artery
C: anterior tibial artery
D: peroneal artery
column2:
Answer: C. anterior tibial artery

Feedback:
The interosseous membrane connects the medial tibia and anterior fibula and separates the muscles of the calf. The ATA and ATV course just anterior to the membrane.

column1:
A patient presents for a venous Doppler evaluation of the lower extremities. The patient is contracted at the knees and hips and unable to extend the legs for the exam. All compression views are normal. Doppler evaluation demonstrates mildly reduced response to distal augmentation in the thigh and continuous flow in the popliteal veins. How will you report these findings?

A: Normal exam with flow changes most likely caused by patient position
B: Normal exam
C: Venous insufficiency is present in the upper thigh vessels bilaterally
D: Probable DVT in the popliteal veins bilaterally
column2:
Answer: A. Normal exam with flow changes most likely caused by patient position

Feedback:
Absence of vein compressibility is the most reliable factor for diagnosing DVT. The patient has normal compression views which indicates the absence of DVT. The contracted position of the patient can cause a more continuous waveform and decrease in augmentation response.

column1:
If the image displayed is a transverse view of the posterior right lower leg, Letter A correspond to:

A: small saphenous vein
B: posterior tibial vein
C: medial gastrocnemius vein
D: great saphenous vein
column2:
Answer: A. small saphenous vein

Feedback:
When imaging the posterior leg, the notch on the transducer should be placed toward the right side of the patient, just like imaging the anterior leg. The right side of the image is lateral and the left side of the image is medial in both imaging views. A. small saphenous vein B. lateral gastrocnemius veins C. medial gastrocnemius veins D. popliteal vein

column1:
Pulsatility Index can be used to describe?

A: the amount of blood vessel recoil
B: the pressure gradient within a vessel
C: the amount of variation in the velocity during the cardiac cycle
D: amount of turbulence within a vessel
column2:
Answer: C. the amount of variation in the velocity during the cardiac cycle

Feedback:
The pulsatility index (PI) can be used to describe the amount of variation in the velocity during the cardiac cycle. High resistance flow demonstrates higher PI values than low resistance flow.

column1:
You are evaluating the subclavian artery and its branches. How can you differentiate the vertebral artery from the thyrocervical and costocervical trunks?

A: the trunk arteries have many branches and lower end diastolic flow velocities
B: the vertebral artery demonstrates flow moving toward the transducer and the trunk arteries demonstrate flow moving away from the transducer
C: the vertebral artery has many branches and lower end diastolic flow velocities
D: the vertebral artery demonstrates flow moving away from the transducer and the trunk arteries demonstrate flow toward the transducer
column2:
Answer: A. the trunk arteries have many branches and lower end diastolic flow velocities

Feedback:
Thyrocervical and Costocervical Trunk arteries can be differentiated from the vertebral arteries by their many branches and lower end-diastolic flow velocities. Flow direction should not be used to identify these vessels because of the many variables in evaluation technique and potential flow abnormalities (like a subclavian steal).

column1:
The attached image is taken from the celiac axis on an abdominal Doppler exam. Which of the following is the most likely finding in this patient when the vessel is re-evaluated after eating?

A: no change in flow pattern
B: decreased resistance with no change in peak velocity
C: increased resistance and increased peak velocity
D: decreased resistance and increased peak velocity
column2:
Answer: A. no change in flow pattern

Feedback:
The celiac artery waveform is usually unchanged after eating in the normal patient. The CA mainly supplies the liver and spleen with blood and these organs are low resistance vascular beds. The fasting waveform and peak velocity on this patient are well within normal limits which indicates a normal celiac axis.

column1:
Which of the following terms is a type of visual disturbance where half of the field of view is disrupted in the eye?

A: ectopia
B: myopia
C: diplopia
D: homonymous hemianopia
column2:
Answer: D. homonymous hemianopia

Feedback:
This visual disturbance can be unilateral or bilateral and is commonly caused by ischemia in the occipital lobe of the brain. Medial or lateral half of the field of view in the eye is "absent" while the other half appears normal.

column1:
Which modality is preferred to diagnose an aortic dissection?

A: CTA
B: Duplex ultrasound
C: Angiography
D: MRI
column2:
Answer: A. CTA

Feedback:
CT scan is preferred for evaluation of AAA and dissection. The exam provides the best detail for the evaluation of aortic size and lumen characteristics. All portions of the aorta can be seen with CT. Ultrasound can be limited by bony anatomy.

column1:
A venous insufficiency exam is performed for a patient with multiple varicosities. The deep system is found to be competent, but there is insufficiency present in the great saphenous vein. Which of the following statements regarding the findings is true?

A: Venous insufficiency is normal in the superficial veins when varicosities are present.
B: The multiple varicosities are primary varicosities.
C: The multiple varicosities are primary and secondary varicosities.
D: The multiple varicosities are secondary varicosities.
column2:
Answer: B. The multiple varicosities are primary varicosities.

Feedback:
Varicosities with superficial venous insufficiency related to deep venous system disease = secondary varicosities Varicosities with incompetent valves causing superficial venous insufficiency and normal deep system = primary varicosities.

column1:
Congestive heart failure is more commonly seen with ____________________, while distal ischemia is more commonly seen with _________________.

A: proximal AV fistula, distal AV fistula
B: distal AV fistula, proximal AV fistula
C: AV fistula, hemodialysis fistula
D: AV malformation, AV fistula
column2:
Answer: A. proximal AV fistula, distal AV fistula

Feedback:
Congestive heart failure is more commonly seen with a proximal AVF, while distal ischemia is more commonly seen with distal AVF.

column1:
How should you prepare the patient to start the examination used to assess the venous refill time using photoplethysmography?

A: Supine legs elevated with sensor at the medial malleolus
B: Supine legs elevated with pressure cuff on thigh
C: Seated at the edge of the table with lower legs dangling, sensor placed at medial malleolus
D: Standing with the sensor at the medial malleolus
column2:
Answer: C. Seated at the edge of the table with lower legs dangling, sensor placed at medial malleolus

Feedback:
A PPG exam is used to calculate the venous refill time (VRT). A sensor is placed at the medial malleolus and the patient is seated at the edge of the table with their legs dangling. The patient performs a series of foot dorsiflexions to empty the blood from the calf. The strip chart recorder demonstrates the changes in blood volume over time. The time that it takes for the line to plateau again is the venous refill time. VRT >20 seconds is normal; <20 seconds indicates insufficiency. If using APG to evaluate the VRT, a cuff is placed on the lower leg and the patient is put in the supine position with the affected leg elevated. Once the flow empties from the leg, the patient stands up and the VRT is recorded as volume returns to maximum levels under the cuff.

column1:
The angular artery is the terminal branch of the

A: Ophthalmic artery
B: Nasal artery
C: Facial artery
D: Anterior cerebral artery
column2:
Answer: C. Facial artery

Feedback:
The angular artery is a terminal branch of the facial artery.

column1:
Which of the following is a common infection that causes skin discoloration in patients with venous insufficiency?

A: stasis dermatitis
B: staphylococcus epidermidis
C: staphylococcus aureus
D: arteritis
column2:
Answer: A. stasis dermatitis

Feedback:
Stasis dermatitis is a common inflammatory skin disease. It is usually the earliest cutaneous sequela of chronic venous insufficiency with venous hypertension and causes discoloration of the ankle and calf.

column1:
A patient is short of breath after walking to the facility for the arterial duplex evaluation. What is the first thing you should do when starting his exam?

A: Have him rest for 20-30 minutes to allow flow in the legs to normalize
B: Take his ankle pressures using the PTA and DPA.
C: Have him lie down and start with the Duplex exam and perform the pressures later.
D: Take his brachial pressures.
column2:
Answer: A. Have him rest for 20-30 minutes to allow flow in the legs to normalize

Feedback:
If the patient does not rest, the exercise from his long walk can affect the results of the expected resting arterial exam. This can lead to overestimation of the severity of the disease identified on the resting exam.

column1:
While scanning through the suboccipital window with midline angulation, the mean flow velocity increases from 55cm/s to 145cm/s at 105mm in depth. What do these findings mean?

A: Significant stenosis is present in the basilar artery
B: Normal velocity changes for the tortuous carotid siphon
C: Significant stenosis is present in the MCA
D: Normal velocity for the posterior cerebral artery
column2:
Answer: A. Significant stenosis is present in the basilar artery

Feedback:
The mean velocity demonstrates a significant increase at a depth of 105mm which is consistent with stenosis in the basilar artery.

column1:
Which of the following statements describes the findings on the image of the cephalic vein in a patient with a brachiocephalic graft?

A: There is a stenosis distal to the graft
B: The patient probably has CHF
C: The measurement indicates normal volume flow rate.
D: There is a stenosis proximal to the graft
column2:
Answer: B. The patient probably has CHF

Feedback:
A volume flow rate over 1200ml/min indicates possible CHF. A high flow rate can indicate volume overload in the venous system.

column1:
The ankle brachial index is commonly performed as a part of which of the following exams?

A: strain gauge plethysmography
B: photoplethysmography
C: exercise testing
D: transcutaneous oximetry
column2:
Answer: C. exercise testing

Feedback:
The ABI is performed at rest and with exercise. If the ABI decreases with exercise, arterial disease is present.

column1:
All of the following terms could be used to describe the attached waveform, except :

A: delayed acceleration
B: reduced deceleration time
C: tardus parvus
D: low resistance
column2:
Answer: B. reduced deceleration time

Feedback:
The waveform demonstrates a slow upstroke (long AT) and late systolic peak that is consistent with a monophasic, tardus parvus waveform. The deceleration is delayed not shortened.

column1:
Which of the following risk factors has the highest incidence of lower extremity thrombus formation?

A: Diabetes
B: Recent hip surgery
C: Polycythemia vera
D: Rotator cuff repair
column2:
Answer: B. Recent hip surgery

Feedback:
Patients who have undergone any type of lower extremity surgery are at the greatest risk of DVT. The 3 factors of Virchow's triad are present in these patients. Usually, these patients are confined to a bed for several days/weeks which leads to stasis. There are significant limitations with using blood thinners immediately following surgery because it would prevent the healing of the surgery and could lead to hemorrhage.

column1:
Which of the following patients would require reactive hyperemia testing instead of exercise testing for a lower extremity exam to evaluate new onset of left leg claudication?

A: Prior history of synthetic graft in the right leg
B: Prior history of DVT 3yrs ago
C: Prior history of untreated multilevel disease in the right leg
D: Prior history of coronary bypass surgery
column2:
Answer: C. Prior history of untreated multilevel disease in the right leg

Feedback:
Reactive hyperemia is the transient increase in blood flow that occurs after a brief period of ischemia. It commonly occurs following the removal of a tourniquet, unclamping an artery during surgery, or after vessel recanalization caused by a device or medication. Reactive Hyperemia Testing uses occlusive cuffs to simulate exercise in patients unable to perform treadmill testing. The procedure is used to simulate exercise for those patients unable to perform treadmill testing for arterial disease. If a patient has known disease in the right leg, the diseased leg may limit the treadmill exercise tolerance for the left leg. Reactive hyperemia testing will better evaluate the new symptoms in the left leg.

column1:
What are the average treadmill settings utilized in exercise testing?

A: 5mph at 12% grade
B: 5mph at 10% grade
C: 2mph at 12% grade
D: 3mph at 8% grade
column2:
Answer: C. 2mph at 12% grade

Feedback:
The average treadmill settings utilized in exercise testing is 2mph speed at 12% grade.

column1:
Velocities in a reversed vein graft are usually _________ at the ________ anastomosis because it is the smaller end of the vein.

A: higher, proximal
B: lower, proximal
C: higher, distal
D: lower, distal
column2:
Answer: A. higher, proximal

Feedback:
Velocities in a reversed vein graft are usually higher at the proximal anastomosis because it is the smaller end of the vein.

column1:
Which of the following correctly describes normal flow in the hepatic vessels?

A: the hepatic artery demonstrates hepatopetal flow
B: the hepatic artery demonstrates hepatofugal flow
C: the portal veins demonstrate hepatofugal flow
D: the hepatic veins demonstrate hepatopetal flow
column2:
Answer: A. the hepatic artery demonstrates hepatopetal flow

Feedback:
Hepatofugal flow is flow that is moving out of the liver. Hepatopetal flow is flow that is moving toward/into the liver. The normal HA and PV demonstrate hepatopetal flow. The hepatic veins demonstrate hepatofugal flow.

column1:
How is Heparin administered to a patient with DVT?

A: orally
B: intra-arterial
C: intravenous
D: subcutaneous injection
column2:
Answer: C. intravenous

Feedback:
DVT treatment can include 5-10 day dose of intravenous Heparin to prevent clot extension, but it does not destroy current thrombus.

column1:
Jaw claudication, headaches and neck pain, and asymmetric brachial blood pressures are all symptoms related to:

A: thoracic outlet syndrome
B: thromboangiitis obliterans
C: Marfan syndrome
D: giant cell arteritis
column2:
Answer: D. giant cell arteritis

Feedback:
Giant Cell Arteritis: Temporal arteritis is associated with headaches and tenderness over the artery. Asymmetric brachial blood pressures are seen in patients with arteritis in extremities. Other symptoms include pain and/or stiffness in the neck, jaw claudication, and visual disturbances. TOS causes upper extremity symptoms. Thromboangiitis obliterans causes lower leg/feet/forearm/hand symptoms. Marfan syndrome is a connective tissue disorder associated with aortic dissection,

column1:
The distal femoral artery becomes the popliteal artery:

A: at the end of the adductor canal
B: in the popliteal fossa
C: at the level of the heads of the gastrocnemius muscles
D: at the level of the medial quadricep tendon
column2:
Answer: A. at the end of the adductor canal

Feedback:
The distal femoral artery becomes the popliteal artery at the end of the adductor canal (Hunter's canal).

column1:
What system setting would result in false-positive findings for reflux in the great saphenous vein?

A: high frame rate
B: high color persistence settings
C: high velocity scale settings
D: high wall filter settings
column2:
Answer: B. high color persistence settings

Feedback:
High color persistence settings may result in false-positive color-flow findings for reflux. Increasing color persistence increases the amount of time the color is displayed on the image as blood flows. Increasing the color persistence can give the illusion of significant reflux because the color display is prolonged. High filter and velocity scale settings would result in less color display. A higher frame rate indicates a higher screen refresh rate and no extension of display of the color.

column1:
Which of the following patients would benefit from a Percutaneous Transluminal Angioplasty?

A: patient with recurrent pulmonary embolism
B: patient with left ICA occlusion
C: patient with chronic iliac DVT
D: patient with 80% stenosis in proximal SFA
column2:
Answer: D. patient with 80% stenosis in proximal SFA

Feedback:
Percutaneous Transluminal Angioplasty is used to open areas of stenosis in an artery. This intervention cannot be performed on a fully occluded vessel.

column1:
Which of the following is unaffected by vasoconstriction and vasodilation in the arterioles?

A: hydrostatic pressure
B: the number of phases of flow in the arterial waveform
C: the amount of diastolic flow in an artery
D: peripheral resistance
column2:
Answer: A. hydrostatic pressure

Feedback:
Hydrostatic pressure is the weight of the column of blood in the vessels when standing. Vasoconstriction will increase peripheral resistance and increase resistance in the feeding arteries. Higher resistance flow will have less antegrade flow in diastole. Vasodilation will decrease peripheral resistance and decrease resistance in the feeding arteries. Lower resistance flow will have more antegrade flow in diastole. A biphasic waveform can become monophasic.

column1:
What blood vessels are demonstrated on the cross section of the penis?

A: helicine arteries
B: urethral arteries
C: dorsal arteries
D: cavernosal arteries
column2:
Answer: D. cavernosal arteries

Feedback:
The image demonstrates a cross section of the penis. The two corpus cavernosum and the corpus spongiosum are identified. The cavernosal artery courses through the corpus cavernosum. Penile Vasculature: Internal iliac arteries give rise to the internal pudendal arteries, bulbourethral artery, and penile arteries. The penile artery branches into the dorsal and cavernosal arteries. The right and left cavernosal arteries enter the right and left CC, respectively. The helicine arteries are multiple tiny branches that penetrate the tissue. Superficial dorsal vein and deep dorsal vein provide the primary venous outflow routes

column1:
A patient presents with persistent calf pain after a recent biking accident and spending a few days on bed rest. After visual inspection of the right leg, what type of vascular exam should be performed?

A: Soft tissue exam to rule out hematoma formation
B: Lower extremity arterial Doppler
C: Lower extremity venous Doppler
D: Segmental pressure exam
column2:
Answer: C. Lower extremity venous Doppler

Feedback:
The significant difference in size demonstrates fluid accumulation in the swollen leg. The recent trauma and few days of stasis while on bed rest most likely caused a DVT to form in the leg.

column1:
Which calf vessel's anatomic course has been referred to as a "stocking seam"?

A: superficial femoral vein (SFV)
B: small saphenous vein (SSV)
C: great saphenous vein (GSV)
D: posterior tibial vein (PTV)
column2:
Answer: B. small saphenous vein (SSV)

Feedback:
The small saphenous vein originates very superficially in the distal posterior calf. It courses midline up the calf to reach the popliteal vein at or near the popliteal fossa. Its course has been said to resemble the seam of a type of pantyhose or stockings.

column1:
What vessel is demonstrated as thrombosed on the image?

A: short saphenous vein
B: basilic vein
C: great saphenous vein
D: cephalic vein
column2:
Answer: C. great saphenous vein

Feedback:
Note the body marker indicator on the image along with the location of the vein just beneath the subcutaneous fat and anterior to the muscular fascia. The lack of a corresponding artery also indicates a superficial vein.

column1:
Which of the following patients would benefit from an exercise segmental pressure exam?

A: Patient with claudication and a resting ABI of 0.95
B: Patient with rest pain and resting ABI 0.4
C: Patient with COPD and resting ABI of 0.9
D: Patient with claudication and resting ABI 0.6
column2:
Answer: A. Patient with claudication and a resting ABI of 0.95

Feedback:
A normal resting ABI can become abnormal with exercise in patients with less severe disease. The patient with COPD would most likely be recommended for reactive hyperemia. An ABI that is less than 0.9 demonstrates PAD is present. The exercise exam will not provide any additional information to assist on the diagnosis of these patients.

column1:
The femoral vein ______________________ the femoral artery.

A: courses posterior to
B: is usually much longer than
C: is harder to compress than
D: courses anterior to
column2:
Answer: A. courses posterior to

Feedback:
The femoral vein courses posterior to the femoral artery.

column1:
Normal extremity arterial flow should demonstrate a _________________acceleration time.

A: rapid
B: prolonged
C: blunted
D: variable
column2:
Answer: A. rapid

Feedback:
Normal extremity arterial flow should demonstrate a rapid acceleration time.

column1:
Which of the following is a symptom usually related to vertebrobasilar disease?

A: Receptive aphasia
B: Dysphagia
C: Expressive aphasia
D: Unilateral visual disturbances
column2:
Answer: B. Dysphagia

Feedback:
Dysphagia refers to difficulty swallowing which is controlled by the brain stem. The vertebrobasilar system supplies the brain stem with blood. Unilateral visual disturbances, receptive aphasia, and expressive aphasia are symptoms related to ICA/MCA disease.

column1:
The image represents what Doppler artifact?

A: Spectral broadening
B: Laminar flow
C: Aliasing
D: Cross Talk
column2:
Answer: C. Aliasing

Feedback:
The waveform wraps around the baseline with the highest velocities displayed incorrectly on the underside of the baseline. This issue is corrected by increasing the scale, use a lower freq. probe, change angle°, adjust approach to decrease distance to vessel, switch to CW.

column1:
What artery courses cephalad on the anterior right side of the trachea to bifurcate at the level of the sternoclavicular joint?

A: subclavian artery
B: vertebral artery
C: carotid artery
D: innominate artery
column2:
Answer: D. innominate artery

Feedback:
There is only one innominate (brachiocephalic) artery on the right side of the body. It originates from the aortic arch and travels cephalad in a position that is to the right and anterior to the trachea. It bifurcates into the right subclavian and vertebral arteries.

column1:
You are evaluating a cephalic vein for bypass graft potential. The vessel diameter is <2mm for its entire length. What should you do next?

A: apply a tourniquet to the forearm and remeasure the cephalic vein segments proximal to the tourniquet
B: report the vein as unusable with a diameter at 2mm and select another upper extremity vein to evaluate for a potential graft
C: report the vein as acceptable for harvest with a maximum diameter of 2mm
D: apply a tourniquet to the upper arm and remeasure the cephalic vein segments distal to the tourniquet
column2:
Answer: D. apply a tourniquet to the upper arm and remeasure the cephalic vein segments distal to the tourniquet

Feedback:
>3mm veins are preferred for graft procedures. The basilic and cephalic veins can be acceptable conduits. When performing the exam, the patient is supine with arm extended at side at a level lower than the heart or in a seated position with hand on lap. Tourniquets can be applied to the proximal extremity in cases where the veins are very small. The veins distal to the tourniquet should dilate to provide an accurate maximum diameter.

column1:
A patient presents for a venous Doppler evaluation of the left lower extremity. Thrombus is identified in the left leg. What should you include in the preliminary report?

A: Amount of exercise the patient performed before symptoms occurred
B: What leg position causes the most pain
C: Location, estimated age and extent of thrombus
D: The peak velocity of the augmentation response proximal and distal to the thrombus
column2:
Answer: C. Location, estimated age and extent of thrombus

Feedback:
When reporting on DVT, the location, estimated age (acute or chronic) and extent of thrombus should be provided. Exercise and augmentation will not be performed with patients with suspected DVT.

column1:
Which of the following is an appropriate indication for an extracranial arterial duplex exam?

A: Follow up exam 6 months after initial diagnosis on patient with <50% ICA stenosis
B: Screening exam on a patient with low Framingham risk scoring and no other assessment
C: Hollenhorst plaque identified on retinal examination
D: 3 month follow up to evaluate a carotid stent in stable patient
column2:
Answer: C. Hollenhorst plaque identified on retinal examination

Feedback:
Hollenhorst plaques are evidence of arterial emboli and are identified on retinal examination. The carotid arteries should be evaluated for the source of the plaque. Screening exams are not recommended for patients with low Framingham risk scoring and no other assessment. They are assumed to be at low risk of disease. 6 month follow up exams are usually reserved for patients with ICA stenosis that is 70% or greater. In the first year after placement of a carotid stent, 1 month baseline exam and a 6 month follow up exam are recommended in stable patients. Regarding the Appropriate Use Criteria as a whole, the list of what is not appropriate is easier to commit to memory than what is appropriate. Review both lists below.

column1:
The most common complication of lower extremity DVT is:

A: phlegmasia cerulea dolens
B: pulmonary embolism
C: phlegmasia cerulea alba
D: valvular incompetence
column2:
Answer: D. valvular incompetence

Feedback:
The presence of DVT damages venous valvular structure and motion and venous insufficiency is the most common sequela seen with DVT. Pulmonary embolism is the most critical complication of a DVT. Phlegmasia alba dolens is an extensive iliofemoral thrombosis, usually due to stasis of uterine blood with recent pelvic surgery or pregnancy. It presents as painful swelling and pallor of the entire leg (milk leg). Phlegmasia cerulea dolens is an uncommon severe form of DVT which results from extensive thrombotic occlusion of the veins of an extremity. It presents as painful "blue" swollen leg. Milk leg becomes Blue leg if untreated.

column1:
What is the preferred manipulation technique that can be used to convert the partial subclavian steal into a complete steal?

A: Re-scan the patient in Fowler's position.
B: Have the patient perform 40 flexion/extension maneuvers of the left arm and then re-evaluate the vertebral flow.
C: Have the patient squeeze a stress ball 40 times in the right arm and then re-evaluate the vertebral flow.
D: Have patient sit up in an exaggerated military stance and then re-evaluate the vertebral flow.
column2:
Answer: B. Have the patient perform 40 flexion/extension maneuvers of the left arm and then re-evaluate the vertebral flow.

Feedback:
By exercising the left arm, the muscles will require more incoming blood flow. The pressure in the distal arm will decrease to allow more flow to travel to the arm. The left vertebral artery is the only supplying vessel for the left arm, due to the subclavian occlusion. This increase in blood flow needs in the left arm will lead to complete reversal of flow in the left vertebral as it tries to keep up with the change in the blood flow needs in the left arm.

column1:
Which of the following is a sign or symptom of chronic DVT?

A: Redness
B: Positive Homan's Sign
C: Warmth
D: Brawny Discoloration
column2:
Answer: D. Brawny Discoloration

Feedback:
Hyperpigmentation refers to brawny discoloration of the distal 1/3 of the calf. It is caused by cell debris and waste accumulating in the tissues outside the veins. Homan's sign refers to calf pain with quick dorsiflexion of the foot. This can be a sign of a DVT with the calf pain relating to the inability to pump out the blood when the muscle is quickly contracted. Swelling, warmth and redness are all related to venous stasis in the vessels below the area of DVT.

column1:
When performing a treadmill test for PAD, exercise is normally performed ________________. When performing a reactive hyperemia exam for PAD, the cuffs are inflated ________________.

A: with the patient standing, with the patient supine
B: for up to 5 minutes, for up to 5 minutes
C: with the patient supine, with the patient standing
D: for up to 10 minutes, for up to 5 minutes
column2:
Answer: B. for up to 5 minutes, for up to 5 minutes

Feedback:
When performing a treadmill test for PAD, exercise is normally performed for up to 5 minutes. When performing a reactive hyperemia exam for PAD, the cuffs are inflated for up to 5 minutes. Reactive hyperemia is used to simulate exercise in patients that cannot perform exercise. If the patient experiences symptoms, the exam can be terminated earlier.

column1: Use your mouse to move the cursor over the saphenous tributary vein and click to mark the vessel.
column2:
Answer: Unavailable

Feedback:
The saphenous veins course within the saphenous compartment that is formed by the two layers of fascia. Tributary veins are found superficial to the superficial fascia.

column1:
When performing an upper extremity venous Doppler evaluation for thrombosis, which of the following veins should be evaluated on the exam?

A: axillary, cephalic, basilic and brachial veins
B: axillary, brachial and deep forearm veins
C: cephalic, basilic and brachial veins
D: subclavian, axillary, cephalic, basilic and brachial veins
column2:
Answer: D. subclavian, axillary, cephalic, basilic and brachial veins

Feedback:
When performing an upper extremity venous Doppler evaluation for thrombosis, the subclavian, axillary, cephalic, basilic and brachial veins should be evaluated on the exam. The radial and ulnar veins are not typically evaluated at length, similar to the calf veins on a lower extremity exam.

column1:
Which of the following causes an increase in the resistive index(RI) of the distal common carotid artery?

A: aortic valve insufficiency
B: proximal ICA stenosis
C: proximal CCA stenosis
D: Valsalva maneuver
column2:
Answer: B. proximal ICA stenosis

Feedback:
An occlusion of the ICA will lead to increased resistance to the flow in the ipsilateral common carotid artery.

column1:
The images demonstrated are from a mesenteric Doppler exam. The patient was NPO for the exam. The IMA was also evaluated and demonstrated a monophasic, high resistance waveform with a peak velocity of 150cm/s. What do these findings indicate?

A: Normal exam
B: SMA stenosis
C: Normal SMA and IMA flow but a tardus parvus waveform is identified in the celiac axis.
D: Mesenteric ischemia
column2:
Answer: B. SMA stenosis

Feedback:
Mesenteric ischemia requires stenosis or occlusion of at least two of the mesenteric arteries. (Celiac, SMA, or IMA) Only SMA stenosis is present. The other velocities are normal for the IMA and celiac.

column1:
Which of the following indicates single level arterial obstruction in the leg?

A: ABI <0.5
B: velocity of 200cm/s or less
C: ABI >0.5
D: post exercise ankle recovery time 6-12 minutes
column2:
Answer: C. ABI >0.5

Feedback:
ABI >0.5 single level disease; ABI <0.5 multi-level disease

column1:
Bilateral homonymous hemianopia is most commonly seen with:

A: internal carotid artery stenosis
B: subclavian vein thrombosis
C: abdominal aortic aneurysm
D: vertebrobasilar disease
column2:
Answer: D. vertebrobasilar disease

Feedback:
Bilateral visual symptoms are most commonly related to vertebrobasilar disease and reduced flow to the occipital lobes of the brain. Unilateral visual symptoms are most commonly related to ICA disease and decreased flow to the ophthalmic artery.

column1:
If the stenosis/blockage reduces the vessel lumen to 1/4 of the normal vessel area, the velocity of flow at the stenosis must _________________to maintain the flow rate

A: double
B: increase by 400%
C: quadruple
D: decrease by 25%
column2:
Answer: C. quadruple

Feedback:
If the stenosis/blockage reduces the lumen to 1/4 of the normal vessel area, the velocity of flow at the stenosis must quadruple to maintain the flow rate.

column1:
Which of the following is a characteristic of the Doppler waveform obtained distal to the site of stenosis in an ICA that demonstrates string flow on color Doppler?

A: blunted waveform
B: decreased acceleration time
C: spectral window
D: increased diastolic flow
column2:
Answer: A. blunted waveform

Feedback:
Near occlusion of the ICA will lead to low velocity systolic flow with minimal diastolic flow (blunted waveform). Once total occlusion occurs, diastolic flow ceases or flow reversal occurs in the artery just proximal to the occlusion.

column1:
Letter T indicates which of the following structures?

A: right renal artery
B: IVC
C: superior mesenteric artery
D: left renal artery
column2:
Answer: A. right renal artery

Feedback:

column1:
When evaluating the carotid siphon from the submandibular window, the sample depth should be set to value between:

A: 30-50mm
B: 40-60mm
C: 60-80mm
D: 80-110mm
column2:
Answer: C. 60-80mm

Feedback:
The carotid siphon is evaluated when the sample depth is placed at an approximate depth of 70mm (between 60 and 80mm).

column1:
Which of the following is a disadvantage of magnetic resonance angiography compared to Doppler evaluation of the carotid arteries?

A: MRA of the carotid arteries can only be performed on patients who are not diabetic.
B: MRA exams tend to overestimate the level of stenosis present.
C: Patients on anticoagulant therapy cannot undergo an MRA evaluation.
D: Contrast is always required for patients with a history of renal disease.
column2:
Answer: B. MRA exams tend to overestimate the level of stenosis present.

Feedback:
MRA exams tend to overestimate the level of stenosis present when compared to Doppler findings. Contrast may be used on the exam but is not always required. Certain patients are at a higher risk of renal failure from the contrast.

column1:
Which of the following is part of the deep venous system of the forearm?

A: radial vein
B: basilic vein
C: cephalic vein
D: median cubital vein
column2:
Answer: A. radial vein

Feedback:
The basilic, cephalic and median cubital veins are superficial veins of the arm. The radial, ulnar, brachial and axillary veins are deep veins of the arm.

column1:
At ____________________ post-injection, _______________________should be performed to determine the success of a Thrombin injection for pseudoaneurysm treatment.

A: 24-48 hours, color Doppler and ABI evaluation
B: 20 minutes, color Doppler and ABI evaluation
C: 1 hour, CW Doppler evaluation
D: 5 minutes, PPG and ABI evaluation
column2:
Answer: B. 20 minutes, color Doppler and ABI evaluation

Feedback:
At 20 minutes post-injection, color Doppler and ABI evaluation should be performed to determine the success of a Thrombin injection for pseudoaneurysm treatment. The PSA should be filled with echogenic thrombus and no color flow should be present within it. ABI assessment is performed before and after the procedure to confirm the arterial circulation was unaffected. There should be no change in the ABI from the baseline assessment.

column1:
Pressure in the venules does not normally exceed ________________.

A: 0mmHg
B: 80mmHg
C: 20mmHg
D: 50mmHg
column2:
Answer: C. 20mmHg

Feedback:
Pressure in the venules does not normally exceed 20mmHg.

column1:
Which of the following is associated with spectral broadening?

A: pulsatile flow
B: blunted waveform
C: laminar flow
D: increased bandwidth
column2:
Answer: D. increased bandwidth

Feedback:
Bandwidth refers to the difference between the highest and lowest frequencies in the pulse. Laminar flow has a narrow bandwidth of reflected signals because the blood cells are all moving at relatively the same speed. This allows for the display of a spectral window on the tracing. With turbulent flow, the blood cells are moving at many different velocities. This produces a wide bandwidth of reflected signals and the spectral window fills in.

column1:
A patient presents for a follow up of stent placement in the SMA due to stenosis. How will you determine if the procedure was a success?

A: at least a 70% reduction in velocity compared to pre-stented velocities
B: peak velocities should be reduced compared to pre-procedure levels, but will still appear elevated when compared to normal velocities of non-stenotic arterial flow
C: at least a 50% reduction in velocity compared to pre-stented velocities
D: peak velocities should have returned to normal levels related to non-stenotic arterial flow
column2:
Answer: B. peak velocities should be reduced compared to pre-procedure levels, but will still appear elevated when compared to normal velocities of non-stenotic arterial flow

Feedback:
When a stent is used to treat an arterial obstruction it helps to maintain a patent lumen, but will result in elevated velocities compared to expected normal non-stenotic velocities. Follow up exams should demonstrate reasonable velocity reduction compared to pre-stented levels, but normal velocities should not be expected.

column1:
Which of the following vessels will be abnormally dilated with significant portal HTN?

A: main portal vein and hepatic veins
B: hepatic veins only
C: left gastric vein and hepatic veins
D: main portal vein and left gastric vein
column2:
Answer: D. main portal vein and left gastric vein

Feedback:
The left gastric vein (AKA coronary vein) dilates with significant portal HTN. It normally drains into the splenic vein near the portal confluence and is best evaluated in sagittal plane near porta hepatis. A dilated, patent umbilical vein is also a sign of significant portal HTN. It is best evaluated in the sagittal plane to the left of midline at the level of the ligamentum teres. It acts as a reservoir for portal blood with nowhere to go.

column1:
Which of the following describes how to properly set the color Doppler gain levels for an image?

A: increase the gain until blossoming is noted and then decrease to just below that level
B: starting at a low setting, increase the gain until only one color is displayed throughout the image
C: increase the gain until aliasing is noted and then decrease to just below that level
D: starting at a high setting with aliasing displayed, decrease the gain until only one color is displayed throughout the image
column2:
Answer: A. increase the gain until blossoming is noted and then decrease to just below that level

Feedback:
Proper color Doppler gain setting involves increasing the gain until blossoming is noted and then decreasing to just below that level. Aliasing is best resolved by increasing the color scale, not gain.

column1:
The Doppler evaluation displays a waveform from the hepatic artery and the portal vein. Which of the following statements is correct?

A: The tracing is normal because the flow is in opposing directions
B: The tracing is abnormal because baseline is inverted incorrectly
C: The tracing is abnormal because vessels should both display hepatofugal flow
D: The tracing is abnormal because vessels should both display hepatopetal flow
column2:
Answer: D. The tracing is abnormal because vessels should both display hepatopetal flow

Feedback:
The hepatic artery and the portal vein both carry blood into the liver (hepatopetal). The attached image demonstrates flow reversal in the portal vein which is a common sign of portal HTN.

column1:
A patient presents for ultrasound evaluation of a PICC line. The findings on the image are most suggestive of:

A: normal catheter demonstrated in the right internal jugular vein
B: thrombus formation around the catheter in the right internal jugular vein
C: thrombus formation around the catheter in the right subclavian vein
D: thrombus formation around the catheter in the left internal jugular vein
column2:
Answer: B. thrombus formation around the catheter in the right internal jugular vein

Feedback:
The image demonstrates the longitudinal view of the subclavian vein on the posterior image. The transverse view of the IJV demonstrates a hypoechoic mass surrounding the catheter. The CCA is medial to the IJV = right side.

column1:
According to the attached color map, what color will the blood be demonstrated that is moving toward the probe at an average velocity of 100cm/s?

A: yellow
B: red
C: white/light blue
D: blue
column2:
Answer: C. white/light blue

Feedback:
The speed of the blood exceeds the scale displayed therefore aliasing of the color display will occur. Normally flow toward the probe would be red, IF the average velocity is less than 0.43 m/s. Flow moving at 1.0 m/s (100cm/s) exceeds the top limit of 0.43 m/s which will cause aliasing. The white or light blue color of the blue color bar will be seen and is related to the "wrap around" of the color display.

column1:
Which of the following correctly describes proper behavior related to a sterile field?

A: Everyone in the procedure room should be in sterile PPE
B: Sterile individuals should pass by each other while facing each other
C: Sterile individuals should only touch other sterile items
D: Sterile gloves must be used to open the procedure tray
column2:
Answer: C. Sterile individuals should only touch other sterile items

Feedback:
Everyone in the procedure room cannot be in sterile PPE. Opening non-sterile packaging for sterile items should be done by someone not dressed in sterile attire/gloves. Touching a non-sterile item with sterile gloves renders you "contaminated". Sterile packages can be opened with non-sterile gloves, but if any items inside the tray will be touched, sterile gloves must be used. Sterile gloves must be used to remove items for the tray to prepare them (lidocaine, needle, syringe, biopsy instrument). Sterile individuals should pass back-to-back with other sterile individuals to prevent contamination.

column1:
Which of the following statements regarding the radial artery is true?

A: It supplies the majority of blood to the hand.
B: It can be an alternative coronary bypass graft when the great saphenous vein is not an option.
C: It is the upper extremity artery most commonly affected by atherosclerosis.
D: Requires normal results from Tinel test prior to harvesting.
column2:
Answer: B. It can be an alternative coronary bypass graft when the great saphenous vein is not an option.

Feedback:
The radial artery is commonly used for CABG surgery. The ulnar artery supplies the majority of blood to the hand. The Tinel test evaluates suspected carpal tunnel syndrome.

column1:
Which statement best describes the Doppler tracing from a bypass graft in the lower extremity?

A: There is increased velocity and spectral broadening present which indicates significant stenosis is present.
