B: There is normal velocity flow with mild spectral broadening present which indicates no significant disease is present.
C: There is increased velocity and post stenotic turbulence present which indicates significant stenosis is present.
D: The waveform is normal velocity and appearance for the lower extremity graft segment being evaluated.
column2:
Answer: A. There is increased velocity and spectral broadening present which indicates significant stenosis is present.

Feedback:
The velocity appears to be approximately 300cm/s which is abnormal. The absence of the spectral window indicates spectral broadening.

column1:
A 30yr old male patient presents with persistent right calf pain with exercise. The image displayed is from the right popliteal artery with the foot in the neutral and flexed position. These findings are most suggestive of:

A: popliteal entrapment
B: Leriche syndrome
C: Buerger disease
D: anterior compartment syndrome
column2:
Answer: A. popliteal entrapment

Feedback:
Popliteal entrapment is a compression disorder that most commonly occurs with flexion of the foot. Note the normal waveform disappears completely with flexion of the foot/calf muscle contraction.

column1:
When a water hammer pulse is identified in the carotid arteries, what cardiac complication should be suspected?

A: severe mitral regurgitation
B: severe tricuspid regurgitation
C: severe aortic regurgitation
D: severe mitral stenosis
column2:
Answer: C. severe aortic regurgitation

Feedback:
Blood moves back through the aortic valve into the left ventricle during diastole. It is usually caused by a diseased valve, congenital defects and chamber or aortic dilatation. Significant regurgitation results in volume overload in the chamber receiving the retrograde blood flow. With aortic regurgitation, volume overload occurs in the left ventricle. Pulsus bisferiens can occur in the carotid arteries. The waveforms will demonstrate double systolic peaks. Severe regurgitation is associated with diastolic flow reversal in the carotid arteries (Water Hammer Pulse).

column1:
You perform a bilateral venous Doppler due to bilateral swelling and pain in the legs. Both common femoral vein waveforms look like the tracing on the image. These findings are most consistent with:

A: IVC obstruction
B: peripheral arterial disease
C: severe tricuspid regurgitation
D: May Thurner syndrome
column2:
Answer: C. severe tricuspid regurgitation

Feedback:
Cardiac pulsatility in the lower extremity veins can be related to venous HTN. This is caused by CHF, renal dysfunction and severe tricuspid regurgitation. May Thurner syndrome only affects the left leg due to compression of the left iliac vein. IVC obstruction would cause continuous flow in the lower extremities.

column1:
Why are serial exams necessary on patients prescribed Streptokinase?

A: to evaluate for the reduction of plaque formation in the arteries
B: to evaluate for the recanalization of an acute arterial occlusion
C: to evaluate for evidence of resolving arteritis due to Buerger disease
D: to evaluate for the presence of newly formed collateral arterial pathways
column2:
Answer: B. to evaluate for the recanalization of an acute arterial occlusion

Feedback:
Streptokinase is a lytic agent used to treat an acute arterial occlusion and significant, extensive acute DVT. Serial exams are necessary to evaluate recanalization and tailor the treatment accordingly.

column1:
When performing a photoplethysmography exam for arterial insufficiency, where should you place the sensor?

A: distal pad of the great toe
B: dorsal aspect of the great toe
C: medial distal calf
D: groin
column2:
Answer: A. distal pad of the great toe

Feedback:
Venous Insufficiency is assessed by placing the sensor on the distal calf. Arterial disease is assessed by placing the sensor on the pads of the toes.

column1:
An abnormal renal-aortic ratio would be a ratio ___________.

A: <3.5
B: >1.8
C: <1.8
D: >3.5
column2:
Answer: D. >3.5

Feedback:
The renal aortic ratio is calculated by dividing the peak renal artery velocity by the peak velocity in the aorta. A value <3.5 indicates a normal exam. This ratio means that the blood in the renal arteries is moving 3.5 times faster than the blood in the aorta. Higher ratios mean higher renal artery velocities = stenosis.

column1:
Which method is preferred to evaluate suspected popliteal entrapment?

A: pulse volume recordings of the leg arteries with the patient supine and standing
B: PPG tracing of the digits at rest and during treadmill exercise
C: PPG tracing of the digits at rest and during dorsiflexion
D: segmental pressures before and after treadmill exercise
column2:
Answer: C. PPG tracing of the digits at rest and during dorsiflexion

Feedback:
Evaluation of a patient with suspected popliteal entrapment should be performed using PPG sensors to record changes in flow during calf contraction. Active plantar or dorsiflexion of the foot can be performed to cause controlled calf muscle contraction.

column1:
The attached picture is most suggestive of what acute vascular disorder?

A: Unilateral DVT
B: Bilateral DVT
C: Peripheral arterial disease
D: Congestive heart failure
column2:
Answer: A. Unilateral DVT

Feedback:
Significant edema is evident in the subcutaneous tissues. This is most commonly associated with Acute DVT. Peripheral arterial disease is a chronic disorder. CHF or bilateral DVT would cause bilateral swelling.

column1:
The paratibial perforators connect:

A: the small saphenous vein and popliteal vein
B: the small saphenous vein and posterior tibial veins
C: the great saphenous vein and posterior tibial veins
D: the great saphenous vein and the anterior tibial veins
column2:
Answer: C. the great saphenous vein and posterior tibial veins

Feedback:
The paratibial perforators connect the GSV and the PTVs.

column1:
Which of the following will result in a low resistance antegrade external carotid artery waveform with increased antegrade flow in diastole?

A: ipsilateral mid common carotid artery stenosis
B: temporal arteritis
C: ipsilateral mid common carotid artery occlusion
D: contralateral subclavian stenosis
column2:
Answer: A. ipsilateral mid common carotid artery stenosis

Feedback:
The ECA normally demonstrates a high resistance flow pattern with minimal antegrade diastolic flow. A proximal stenosis will cause reduced resistance in the flow/waveforms distally. Stenosis proximal to the ECA (in the CCA) or in the proximal ECA will cause lower resistance ECA flow with increased antegrade flow in diastole. If the ipsilateral ICA is occluded, these same changes will occur.

column1:
Which of the following is NOT a potential acute complication of a liver transplant?

A: hepatic artery thrombosis
B: cirrhosis
C: portal vein stenosis
D: portal thrombosis
column2:
Answer: B. cirrhosis

Feedback:
Cirrhosis is a chronic liver disease. Thrombosis and extrinsic vessel narrowing/compression are acute complications that can occur in a liver transplant.

column1:
The following pressures were obtained in a patient during a segmental pressure exam. Where is the disease, if any? Right: PTA 136mmHg, DPA, 130mmHg, Calf 136mmHg, Low Thigh 144mmHg, High Thigh 160mmHg, Brachial 130mmHg Left: PTA 98mmHg, DPA 85mmHg, Calf 100mmHg, Low Thigh 114mmHg, High Thigh 128mmHg, Brachial 126mmHg

A: iliac disease left leg, normal right leg
B: normal left leg, medial calcification right leg
C: aortic disease or left iliac disease
D: left calf artery disease, normal right leg
column2:
Answer: A. iliac disease left leg, normal right leg

Feedback:
The left ABI value is reduced at 0.75. When using 4 cuffs, the high thigh pressure is normally 30-40mmHg higher than the brachial pressure. The other pressures in the leg do not demonstrate a significant drop between consecutive segments. There is iliac disease that is causing the decrease in the pressures in the entire leg. If aortic disease was present, both legs would be affected.

column1:
When evaluating flow in the superior mesenteric artery, what is the most important history question to ask the patient?

A: Do you have diabetes?
B: Do you have high blood pressure that cannot be treated with medication?
C: Do you have a pain in your abdomen that is associated with breathing motions?
D: When was your last meal?
column2:
Answer: D. When was your last meal?

Feedback:
In order to evaluate the SMA waveform, it is important to know the activity level in the digestive tract. Pre-prandial (NPO) = no digestion, high resistance flow with minimal diastolic flow. Post prandial - digestion increases inflow needs, low resistance flow with mild diastolic flow Untreatable HTN is associated with renal artery stenosis. Abdomen pain with expiration is associated with celiac artery compression.

column1:
Which of the following correctly describes carotid dissection?

A: Dissection is more common in the Circle of Willis than the extracranial arteries
B: Commonly associated with Paget Schroetter syndrome
C: Dissection is more common in the extracranial ICA than the intracranial ICA
D: Always associated with trauma
column2:
Answer: C. Dissection is more common in the extracranial ICA than the intracranial ICA

Feedback:
Dissection is more common in the extracranial ICA than the intracranial ICA

column1:
What causes the artifact demonstrated on the image?

A: evaluating vessels next to adjacent highly reflective structures
B: bidirectional flow in vessel
C: increased filter settings
D: lack of perpendicular incidence between the transducer and blood flow
column2:
Answer: A. evaluating vessels next to adjacent highly reflective structures

Feedback:
Cross talk is a type of mirror imaging artifact. It is caused by adjacent highly reflective structures, receiver gain settings too high or incident Doppler angle near 90°.

column1:
The Valsalva maneuver is most useful when trying to assess which of the following?

A: Venous insufficiency
B: DVT
C: median arcuate syndrome
D: ECA/ICA differentiation
column2:
Answer: A. Venous insufficiency

Feedback:
In the lower extremities, the Valsalva maneuver increases intra-abdominal pressure and inhibits normal venous return to the heart. If the venous valves are incompetent, flow reversal will be seen while the maneuver is applied.

column1:
Aside from the carotid artery, what other vessels are commonly affected by fibromuscular dysplasia (FMD)?

A: renal and mesenteric arteries
B: jugular veins
C: small arteries of the foot and ankle
D: small arteries of the hand and wrist
column2:
Answer: A. renal and mesenteric arteries

Feedback:
If FMD is identified in the carotid arteries, the renal and mesenteric arteries should also be evaluated for FMD. Stenosis caused by atherosclerosis usually affects the origin of the artery, while FMD usually causes stenosis in the mid/distal artery.

column1:
What Doppler artifact is demonstrated on the tracing?

A: Clutter
B: Cross talk
C: Aliasing
D: Ghosting
column2:
Answer: B. Cross talk

Feedback:
Cross Talk is a type of mirror imaging. It is caused by adjacent highly reflective structures, receiver gain too high or incident Doppler angle near 90°.

column1:
What should be done to adjust and improve the color Doppler displayed on the image?

A: Increase velocity scale
B: Increase PRF
C: Steer box
D: Decrease sample volume
column2:
Answer: D. Decrease sample volume

Feedback:
Color Doppler processing ability improves as the size of the area evaluated is decreased. The smaller sample size allows faster frame rates and blood flow display. Increasing color velocity and PRF would decrease the amount of color currently displayed.

column1:
Which extremity arterial evaluation technique can be described as the placement of multiple cuffs around the extremity that measure the volume changes beneath them?

A: Segmental Pressures
B: Impedance plethysmography
C: Pulse volume recording
D: Photoplethysmography
column2:
Answer: C. Pulse volume recording

Feedback:
Pulse volume recording is a type of plethysmography exam used to evaluate the arterial inflow of an extremity. Partially inflated cuffs placed on the extremity at different levels record the volume changes on a strip chart recorder. The higher the amplitude of the analog signal, the better the arterial inflow under that cuff. Segmental pressure exams measure the pressure in the artery under the cuff.

column1:
If the peak velocity in a transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt at the portal vein anastomosis is 50cm/s and the mid graft velocity is 25cm/s, what is indicated?

A: The mid graft velocity is very low and indicates impending failure of the shunt.
B: There is approximately a 50% stenosis at the portal anastomosis.
C: There is most likely an occlusion in the graft at the proximal end.
D: The velocities indicate normal flow within the shunt.
column2:
Answer: A. The mid graft velocity is very low and indicates impending failure of the shunt.

Feedback:
Flow velocity <50cm/s in the intrahepatic shunt indicates impending failure.

column1:
Which of the following Doppler characteristics will be identified at an 80% popliteal artery stenosis?

A: Increased diastolic flow reversal
B: Increased acceleration time
C: Loss of the retrograde flow component in diastole
D: Prominent spectral window
column2:
Answer: C. Loss of the retrograde flow component in diastole

Feedback:
The diastolic flow reversal will become continuous antegrade flow throughout the cycle. (multiphasic to monophasic). The flow will reach the peak velocity faster at the site of stenosis than in a normal vessel = reduced/faster acceleration time. The acceleration time will increase distally as the stenosis increases and resistance drops distally to encourage more flow. Turbulence caused by the stenosis results in filling in of the spectral window.

column1:
How do the microbubbles in an ultrasound contrast agent change the image?

A: increase the reflectivity of flowing blood
B: increase vasodilation to move the bubbles more quickly so they are visible to ultrasound
C: microbubbles cannot be used to evaluate arterial flow due to embolism risk
D: increase the resistance to flowing blood to make it more visible
column2:
Answer: A. increase the reflectivity of flowing blood

Feedback:
Microbubbles in an ultrasound contrast agent increase the reflectivity of flowing blood.

column1:
The Appropriate Use Criteria were developed:

A: to identify physicians and facilities that need further education on ordering procedures
B: by a collaboration of insurance companies seeking to streamline covered services
C: to provide guidance for when to use vascular ultrasound as a secondary exam to confirm a diagnosis
D: while considering vascular ultrasound or physiologic testing by itself as a reasonable exam choice
column2:
Answer: D. while considering vascular ultrasound or physiologic testing by itself as a reasonable exam choice

Feedback:
The Appropriate Use Criteria for vascular ultrasound and physiologic testing was developed through the collaboration of several vascular ultrasound and medical societies to improve efficiency and effectiveness of patient diagnosis and treatment. The most common indications given for vascular ultrasound and physiologic testing were scored for appropriate use. Not all exam indications were evaluated. The indications were scored while considering vascular ultrasound or physiologic testing by itself as a reasonable exam choice. Three categories used to classify indications: 7-9 points: Generally appropriate indication 4-6 points: May be appropriate or reasonable indication; situational consideration 1-3 points: Inappropriate or unreasonable indication If there is more than one indication for an exam, the indication with the highest score should be used to determine appropriate use

column1:
Which of the following statements is true regarding a PPG exam for venous insufficiency?

A: Tourniquets are used in conjunction with PPG exam to depict the extent of DVT.
B: Waveforms obtained for a venous PPG exam are similar to those obtained in a venous duplex exam.
C: The best readings with severe insufficiency will be obtained at an ulceration site.
D: Prolonged venous refill time (>20s) indicates competent venous valves.
column2:
Answer: D. Prolonged venous refill time (>20s) indicates competent venous valves.

Feedback:
PPG sensors must be placed on areas of intact skin. Tourniquets are used in conjunction with PPG exam to depict the extent of venous insufficiency. Longer VRT values are desired, short refill times indicate reflux of venous outflow.

column1:
A patient is undergoing balloon angioplasty of the left common femoral artery. The physician just completed the first expansion of the balloon and asks you to check the flow with Doppler. Where is the preferred location to evaluate flow?

A: proximal superficial femoral artery
B: dorsalis pedis or posterior tibial artery
C: popliteal artery
D: distal superficial femoral artery
column2:
Answer: B. dorsalis pedis or posterior tibial artery

Feedback:
The physician is trying to increase flow distal to the obstruction. It is most important to evaluate flow in the most distal areas to evaluate improvement in overall perfusion of the leg.

column1:
An asymptomatic patient demonstrates a normal left ABI, but the left tibial arteries demonstrate a monophasic waveform with sharp upstroke and rapid deceleration. What is the most likely cause for this finding?

A: Scale settings set too high to detect low velocity flow reversal.
B: Filter settings set too low to detect diastolic flow reversal.
C: The patient should be evaluated for popliteal entrapment.
D: Raynaud syndrome
column2:
Answer: A. Scale settings set too high to detect low velocity flow reversal.

Feedback:
The rapid acceleration and deceleration indicate a high resistance waveform that would be normal in the tibial arteries. The diastolic flow reversal component can be very small due to low velocity and a short duration of the flow. Decreased scale and filter settings will aid in visualization of this component of the waveform.

column1:
What system control can reduce the appearance of clutter on a color Doppler image?

A: increasing persistence
B: decreasing color scale
C: increasing color threshold
D: decreasing color priority
column2:
Answer: D. decreasing color priority

Feedback:
Reducing color on an image can be done by decreasing color gain, increasing the PRF/color scale, reducing color persistence, increasing rejection, increasing wall filter, reducing color threshold and reducing color priority. Color Priority: Adjusts the threshold setting; Determines if a pixel is displayed as shade of gray, black or white or as a color; Lower threshold settings display less color (lower color priority); Higher threshold settings display more color (higher color priority); Necessary to minimize color flash(clutter) and improper placement of color on vessel walls

column1:
When evaluating a suspected stenosis in the lower extremity arterial system, the peak systolic velocity is documented on the waveforms obtained. What other two factors should be reported to determine the severity of the stenosis?

A: resistive index and diastolic flow direction
B: resistive index and velocity ratio
C: pulsatility index and systolic flow direction
D: velocity ratio and diastolic flow direction
column2:
Answer: D. velocity ratio and diastolic flow direction

Feedback:
The velocity ratio is used to compare the PSV proximal to the stenosis to the PSV at the stenosis. A ratio greater than 2 indicates stenosis. A triphasic pattern has two components of diastolic flow. Retrograde flow occurs immediately after systole ends followed by a small amount of antegrade flow. When the second component of diastolic flow is absent, the waveform is considered biphasic. Monophasic flow demonstrates antegrade flow in systole and diastole. Resistive and Pulsatility indices are not commonly used to report the severity of stenosis in the extremities. The RI is necessary when evaluating parenchymal perfusion in an organ like the kidney or liver.

column1:
A curvilinear transducer can be used to better evaluate:

A: flow in the distal posterior tibial artery
B: flow in the anterior tibial artery
C: varicosities covering a large area
D: deep veins in obese patients
column2:
Answer: D. deep veins in obese patients

Feedback:
Most DVT exams will be performed with a 5-7MHz linear transducer. A curvilinear transducer may be necessary to evaluate obese patients because of the lower frequency range and wider field of view in the near field compared to a sector array. Superficial structures are best evaluated with a high frequency linear transducer.

column1:
Which statement is true about the image?

A: The red arrows are pointing to the anterior wall of the Baker's cyst and the associated enhancement.
B: The red arrows are pointing to the posterior wall of the Baker's cyst and the associated enhancement.
C: The red arrows are pointing to the anterior wall of the Baker's cyst and the associated shadowing.
D: The red arrows are pointing to the posterior wall of the Baker's cyst and the associated shadowing.
column2:
Answer: A. The red arrows are pointing to the anterior wall of the Baker's cyst and the associated enhancement.

Feedback:
The popliteal fossa is evaluated with a posterior transducer approach. The posterior border of the Baker's cyst is demonstrated most anterior on the image. The anterior border of the Baker's cyst is demonstrated posteriorly on the image with increased posterior enhancement.

column1:
A patient presents with an order for a lower extremity venous Doppler exam to rule out DVT. This patient should be evaluated in the _____________ position in order to _______________.

A: supine, increase hydrostatic pressure
B: semi-erect, reduce hydrostatic pressure
C: semi-erect, increase hydrostatic pressure
D: supine, reduce hydrostatic pressure
column2:
Answer: C. semi-erect, increase hydrostatic pressure

Feedback:
The semi-erect position will also increase intraabdominal pressure which slows lower extremity venous return. This will fill the veins of the legs with blood making the US and Doppler evaluation much easier.

column1:
Which of the following is part of the Sonographer's responsibilities when assisting with an endovenous ablation?

A: inject the area around the saphenofemoral junction with anesthetic
B: performing the groin puncture and inserting the proximal end of the catheter into the vein that will be treated by the physician
C: place the patient in the Trendelenburg position to prepare for catheter insertion
D: review patient records and ultrasound images to confirm the diagnosis and the leg that will be treated
column2:
Answer: D. review patient records and ultrasound images to confirm the diagnosis and the leg that will be treated

Feedback:
Puncturing the vein and performing the catheter insertion fall outside the scope of practice of a Sonographer. The ablation procedure must be performed by the physician. Sonographer responsibilities include: review patient records and ultrasound exam to confirm the diagnosis and the leg that will be treated confirm patient understanding of procedure and the leg and vein to be treated explain the procedure to the patient provide the informed consent form to the patient to read (ultimately the patient should sign the form after the patient has had the chance to ask the physician any related questions about the form) place the patient in the reverse Trendelenburg position and perform any necessary preliminary images map the position of vein determine area of catheter access and mark set up the supplies for the procedure cover appropriate transducer with a sterile probe cover operate the ultrasound machine controls outside the sterile field, as directed by the physician apply dressing to the access point confirm that the patient understands the aftercare instructions given by the physician disinfect the room and the ultrasound equipment after the procedure discard all used needles and catheters into the biohazard container

column1:
According to the Appropriate Use Criteria, which of the following is an inappropriate indication for an aortic duplex exam?

A: Lower extremity claudication
B: 1 month follow up after endograft placement
C: Pulsatile abdominal mass
D: Fever of unknown origin
column2:
Answer: D. Fever of unknown origin

Feedback:
An isolated findings of a fever of unknown origin is not an appropriate indication for any vascular ultrasound exam. It is a non-specific symptom that has rarely been associated with vascular disease. Unilateral lower extremity symptoms can be caused by iliac artery disease and bilateral symptoms can be related to aortic disease. A pulsatile abdominal mass is a clinical indication of AAA. If endograft placement is performed to treat an AAA, the baseline exam should be performed within 1 month of the procedure.

column1:
Which of the following is NOT a part of the information required on an informed consent form for an interventional vascular procedure?

A: percentage of patients that have experienced complications after the same procedure at the facility
B: possible complications that the patient may experience
C: description of the procedure
D: signature line to indicate patient consent
column2:
Answer: A. percentage of patients that have experienced complications after the same procedure at the facility

Feedback:
The informed consent must include a description of the procedure and list the possible complications that the patient may experience. The patient must be of sound mind when giving consent by signature.

column1:
If there is no diastolic flow demonstrated on a Doppler tracing, what will the resistive index be?

A: 0
B: 1.0
C: unable to calculate the RI, not enough information given
D: 0.5
column2:
Answer: B. 1.0

Feedback:
The resistive index is calculated using the peak and end diastolic velocities measured on a Doppler tracing. PSV - EDV / PSV Example: PSV 50cm/s, EDV 0cm/s; 50 - 0 / 50 = 1.0

column1:
A ventilation quotient scan is used to evaluate a patient with suspected:

A: ICA occlusion
B: portal vein thrombosis
C: DVT
D: pulmonary embolism
column2:
Answer: D. pulmonary embolism

Feedback:
Ventilation Quotient Scan (VQ Scan) is used to evaluate the lung vasculature for filling defects and abnormalities in perfusion. It is most commonly performed to assess possible pulmonary embolism. A VQ scan can also be used to evaluate regional lung function in people with advanced pulmonary disease. If the VQ scan demonstrates a possible PE, a venous duplex exam and/or echo will be ordered to identify the source of the thrombus.

column1:
Which type of image resolution makes it possible to differentiate the intimal media thickness from the outer arterial wall?

A: temporal
B: lateral
C: elevational
D: axial
column2:
Answer: D. axial

Feedback:
Axial resolution refers to the machine's ability to resolve structures that are parallel to the ultrasound beam. Measuring the anterior-posterior thickness of a structure, such as the wall thickness of an artery or the thickness of the gallbladder wall, relies on the axial resolution of the system.

column1:
If a renal artery Doppler exam demonstrates stenosis and stenosis is identified on angiography evaluation, the US results are described as:

A: true negative
B: false negative
C: false positive
D: true positive
column2:
Answer: D. true positive

Feedback:
If the current exam is negative but the gold standard is positive = false negative results on current exam If the current exam is positive but the gold standard is negative= false positive results on current exam If the current exam is negative and the gold standard is negative = true negative results on current exam If the current exam is positive and the gold standard is positive = true positive results on current exam

column1:
When the velocity of flow is calculated for vessels evaluated on a TCD exam, a ____________ degree angle of incidence is assumed.

A: 30
B: 0
C: 45
D: 60
column2:
Answer: B. 0

Feedback:
When the velocity of flow is calculated for vessels evaluated on a TCD exam, a 0-degree angle of incidence is assumed.

column1:
Which of the following is a complication of series stenosis in an artery?

A: velocity and stenosis can be underestimated at the second stenosis
B: velocity and stenosis can be overestimated at the first stenosis
C: velocity and stenosis can be underestimated at the first stenosis
D: velocity and stenosis can be overestimated at the second stenosis
column2:
Answer: A. velocity and stenosis can be underestimated at the second stenosis

Feedback:
Stenoses in a series - multiple areas of stenosis in the same vessel will increase the resistance to flow more than a single stenotic area; velocity and stenosis can be underestimated at the second stenosis because of the drop in velocity after the first stenosis Stenoses in parallel - stenosis in different vessels coursing in the same direction (collaterals); has a lesser effect on resistance to flow than series stenoses

column1:
Which of the following imaging parameters is responsible for the ability to resolve two atheromas that are side by side in an artery?

A: lateral resolution
B: dynamic range
C: axial resolution
D: contrast resolution
column2:
Answer: A. lateral resolution

Feedback:
Lateral resolution allows for the differentiation of objects that are perpendicular to the beam. Lateral resolution allows for the differentiation of objects that are side by side.

column1:
Prior to any invasive procedure, the patient should be educated about the procedure, expected outcomes and any possible complications. The patient will be required to sign a document stating their understanding of these topics in order to proceed with the procedure. This form is called:

A: informed consent form
B: litigation waiver
C: health insurance waiver
D: patient acceptance form
column2:
Answer: A. informed consent form

Feedback:
Prior to any invasive procedure, the patient should be educated about the procedure, expected outcomes and any possible complications. The patient will be required to sign a document stating their understanding of these topics in order to proceed with the procedure. This form is called an informed consent.
